title: Lemon balm infusion for mild anxiety
subtitle: A gentle nervine for a busy, anxious mind
excerpt: A hot infusion of dried lemon balm leaves, taken for mild anxiety, nervous tension, or the restless state that comes with an overactive mind. One of the most pleasant-tasting nervine herbs in the western tradition, with a gentle lemon-and-honey flavour.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), melissa entry, nervine, carminative, and mild sedative action. Paracelsus and early European apothecary tradition, melissa as a herb for the heart and nervous system. EMA HMPC monograph on Melissa officinalis folium (2013), confirms traditional use for mild symptoms of mental stress and difficulty falling asleep.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Dried lemon balm loses volatile oils quickly, buy in small quantities (50 g at a time) and store in a sealed tin. Brew each cup fresh.

recipeTools.notes: Any teapot with a lid. The lid is essential.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally used to calm the nervous system, easing tension, anxiety, and restlessness without causing marked sedation.

content.text: A cup of lemon balm tea for the kind of anxiety that keeps the mind busy, circling thoughts, a restless alertness that won't settle, the tension that builds through a stressful afternoon. Lemon balm is the herbal tradition's most approachable

: pleasant-tasting, mild enough to drink daily, and documented in use from the medieval apothecary right through to the EMA's 2013 monograph, which confirms traditional use for mild symptoms of mental stress and difficulty falling asleep. The preparation is a simple covered infusion, the lid is particularly important for lemon balm because the volatile oils responsible for its calming action are the first thing to escape if the cup is left uncovered.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Thyroid medication: lemon balm has traditionally been noted to have mild thyroid-modulating activity. If you are taking thyroxine or other thyroid medications, discuss regular lemon balm use with your prescriber, the interaction is documented in the literature though it is unlikely to be significant at one or two cups a day.

Pregnancy: lemon balm infusion at normal tea strength is widely regarded as safe. As with all medicinal herbs during pregnancy, regular therapeutic-dose use is best discussed with a midwife.

Driving: lemon balm has mild sedative properties at higher doses. If you notice any drowsiness after drinking the tea, do not drive or operate machinery until you know how it affects you personally.

See your doctor if anxiety is interfering with daily life, is accompanied by panic attacks, physical symptoms such as chest pain or palpitations, or has persisted for more than two weeks without obvious cause.

items.prepNote: about 1 heaped tablespoon of loose dried leaves
optional, lemon balm has a natural lemony sweetness

content.text: Place {{dried-lemon-balm-leaves}} of dried lemon balm leaves in the teapot or infuser. Pour {{water}} of just-off-boil water over and put the lid on immediately. Steep for exactly 10 minutes, no longer. Beyond 10 minutes the pleasant lemon flavour tips towards a more bitter, hay-like note, and the volatile oils that carry the nervine action are largely exhausted. Strain and drink slowly.

One cup during or after a period of stress or anxiety, or one cup an hour before bed if sleep is disrupted. Up to three cups a day during an acute anxious period for adults. Lemon balm at normal tea strength is safe for daily use; there are no significant safety concerns with long-term use at culinary-to-medicinal doses. For ongoing anxiety that is affecting daily life, the cup of lemon balm is a supportive measure, see your doctor.

Dried lemon balm loses volatile oils faster than most herbs. Buy in small quantities, keep in a sealed tin or dark glass jar, and check the smell regularly, good lemon balm should smell strongly of lemon when the tin is opened. If the smell is flat or hay-like, the stock needs replacing. Brew each cup fresh.
When NOT to use this remedy

See your doctor for anxiety that is interfering significantly with daily life, accompanied by panic attacks, chest pain, or palpitations, or that has been present for more than two weeks without a clear cause. Discuss with prescriber if taking thyroid medication. The lemon balm cup is for mild, situational anxiety, not for managing an anxiety disorder.
